food_subgroup: Mixed protein hotplate set — grilled fish and beef

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for a combined mackerel-and-beef teppanyaki set. FOODON_03302051 (cooked fish dish) is used as the closest parent for the fish component; the beef component is not separately anchored.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00GJ (Fish and fish products) is the closest defensible parent. No composite teppanyaki set code exists in FoodEx2. The beef component falls under a separate meat category not combined here.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept exists for a composite hotplate mackerel-and-beef set meal.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hotplate set serving comprising approximately 150 g grilled mackerel fillet and 200 g grilled beef slices, totalling approximately 350 g as served.

nutrient_source.title: Mackerel, Atlantic, cooked, dry heat (USDA SR Legacy 15046) and Beef, ground, 80% lean / 20% fat, cooked, pan-browned (USDA SR Legacy 13357); values combined by weighted proportion (43% mackerel, 57% beef by cooked weight)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for a composite hotplate mackerel-and-beef set. Values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for cooked Atlantic mackerel (FDC ID 175139) and cooked 80/20 ground beef (FDC ID 174036) in a 43:57 weight ratio reflecting the 150 g mackerel and 200 g beef serving split. Seasonings (soy sauce, mirin, oil) contribute minor sodium and fat increments estimated from USDA SR Legacy soy sauce (FDC ID 172238) at 5 g per 100 g dish. Confidence is medium due to composite estimation.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Analogous estimate based on grilled fish and lean beef — both protein-dominant, very low carbohydrate foods with GI values in the 0–35 range.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a hotplate mackerel-and-beef set. GI is estimated at 30 based on the analogous category of protein-dominant grilled fish and meat dishes with minimal carbohydrate content (carbohydrate_g ~1.8 per 100 g). The University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 confirm that high-protein, low-carbohydrate cooked meat and fish dishes have GI values in the low range (typically 0–35). Glycemic load per 350 g serving = (30 × 6.3 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 1.9, rounded to 1.1 per 100 g basis; GL per serving = (30 × 6.3) / 100 ≈ 1.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Each 350 g serving contributes approximately 1085 mg sodium, representing nearly half the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce or consume a smaller portion.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and high protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only approximately 6.3 g of carbohydrate per serving and a low estimated glycemic index of 30, this dish produces minimal postprandial glucose excursion and is generally suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, though portion size and total meal carbohydrate context should be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from beef and omega-3 fatty acids from mackerel
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Mackerel provides cardioprotective long-chain omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), which may reduce triglycerides and inflammation, but the beef component contributes saturated fat (~13.7 g per serving); individuals with cardiovascular disease should favour a larger mackerel-to-beef ratio and limit frequency of consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hotplate Mackerel and Beef Set (铁板鲭鱼牛肉套餐; Japanese: ホットプレートサバ＆ビーフセット) is a Japanese-style teppanyaki set meal served on a sizzling iron hotplate. The dish combines a grilled mackerel (saba) fillet and sliced grilled beef, typically seasoned with soy sauce, mirin, garlic, and ginger, then cooked on a cast-iron teppan plate that retains heat and continues cooking the proteins at the table. The mackerel contributes a rich, oily, savoury flavour with a slightly charred exterior and moist interior, while the beef provides a juicy, umami-forward counterpoint. The hotplate presentation is a hallmark of Japanese teppanyaki cuisine and is popular in Singapore's Japanese and Korean-fusion restaurant segment. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (~19.8 g per 100 g), moderate in fat (~11.6 g per 100 g), and very low in carbohydrate (~1.8 g per 100 g), making it a protein-dominant meal. Mackerel is an excellent source of omega-3 long-chain polyunsaturated fatty acids (EPA and DHA), vitamin B12, vitamin D, and selenium. The beef component contributes iron, zinc, and additional B vitamins. Sodium is elevated due to soy-based seasoning (~310 mg per 100 g; ~1085 mg per serving), which is a relevant consideration for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is estimated at 30 (low), and the glycemic load per serving is approximately 1.9, making this dish suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with fish or soy allergies.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for a composite hotplate mackerel-and-beef set meal. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for cooked Atlantic mackerel and cooked 80/20 ground beef in a 43:57 weight ratio. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database category for protein-dominant grilled fish and meat. Confidence is medium rather than low because both component foods are well-characterised in USDA SR Legacy and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA SR Legacy cooked Atlantic mackerel (FDC ID 175139) and cooked 80/20 ground beef (FDC ID 174036) in a 43:57 weight ratio (150 g mackerel : 200 g beef per serving). Sodium is adjusted upward to reflect soy sauce seasoning estimated at 5 g soy sauce per 100 g dish. 2. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero): mackerel and beef contain no dietary fibre; this is naturally zero for a pure protein-and-fat animal-food dish. 3. GI is an analogous estimate of 30 (low) based on the protein-dominant, very-low-carbohydrate profile of grilled fish and meat dishes per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). 4. The input record lists cuisine as Japanese but the serving restaurant is Korean Cuisine - Funan Mall; the dish is a Japanese-style teppanyaki preparation and is classified as Japanese cuisine. Korean local-language names are not mandatory for a Japanese-cuisine dish. 5. trans_fat_g of 0.2 reflects minor naturally occurring trans fats (vaccenic acid) from beef, not industrial trans fats. 6. Vitamin D of 5.1 mcg reflects the well-documented vitamin D content of mackerel per USDA SR Legacy. 7. All -1 sentinel values in per_100g represent nutrients not separately reported or not applicable for this composite estimate; these are not confirmed zeros. 8. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and are not sentinel -1 values. 9. Halal status is unconfirmed as the restaurant and preparation method are not certified halal in the source data.
